top30.title: Reclone: a global research community building equitable access to reagents
top30.abstract: Nature Methods, Published online: 22 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41592-026-03148-0The Reagent Collaboration Network (Reclone) seeks to address the global asymmetry in research and development in biotechnology across the Global South through open sharing of biomaterials, protocols and knowledge. The end goal is to build an international community enabling more sustainable, equitable and decentralized reagent manufacturing. The initiative works through regional distribution hubs and nodes to provide local access to resources and expertise in reagent manufacturing.

top30.title: Genetic diversity of late Neanderthals in northwestern Europe
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 24 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10625-1Genetic sequencing of multiple late Neanderthals living less than 52,500 years ago provides an overview of genetic diversity and demonstrates that even low-coverage nuclear genome data can increase resolution of within-Neanderthal diversity.

top30.title: GW250114 reveals signatures of post-merger black-hole horizon
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 24 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10696-0The observation of a direct wave after the merger of two black holes reveals signatures associated with the remnant black-hole horizon, establishing an observational channel to directly measure frame-dragging effects in black-hole ergospheres and probe the horizon surface gravity.

top30.title: Social technologies need societal alignment
top30.abstract: Nature Computational Science, Published online: 25 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s43588-026-01004-xAlthough social technologies are increasingly co-shaping the public sphere, these systems were not designed as democratic infrastructure. Here, we propose a framework for societal alignment that focuses on procedural fairness to mitigate urgent risks and align emerging technology with societal values.

top30.title: Leveraging longitudinal data to boost statistical power for gene–environment interaction analysis
top30.abstract: Nature Computational Science, Published online: 16 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s43588-026-01002-zThe authors introduce SAGELD, a method that leverages longitudinal data to detect gene–environment interactions with greater statistical power than conventional approaches based on cross-sectional data, improving discovery in large biobank studies.

top30.title: Methane storage using metal–dipyrazolate frameworks
top30.abstract: Nature Materials, Published online: 18 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41563-026-02621-7Reversible phase transformations enable flexible metal–bipyrazolate frameworks to offer high deliverable methane capacities, high stability and recyclability, indicating utility in natural gas storage.

top30.title: Large reasoning models as thinking machines for medicine
top30.abstract: Nature Biomedical Engineering, Published online: 23 June 2026; doi:10.1038/s41551-026-01701-yThis Perspective examines the concept of medical reasoning artificial intelligence for emulating the thinking processes of humans in complex clinical situations, an approach that could bring artificial intelligence closer to being a thinking partner and collaborative tool for clinicians.

top30.title: Briefing Chat: What tickling a chimpanzee can tell us about the evolution of speech
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02059-6Nature staff discuss how apes share a rhythm of laughter, and how AI use may degrade skills in medicine and computer science.

top30.title: The poetry of twilight and the awe-inspiring magic of eclipses: Books in brief
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02055-wAndrew Robinson reviews five of the best science picks.

top30.title: The surprising career parallels between footballers and researchers
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01794-0Sarah Blackford reflects on how early-career scientists and professional football players share similar motivations, pressures and challenges.

top30.title: CRISPR’s next act: the companies editing the epigenome to treat disease
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01976-wA handful of start-up firms are testing therapies that target specific epigenetic markers to treat everything from high cholesterol to a rare muscular disorder.

top30.title: Inside the new political screening that’s stalling NIH grants
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01924-8Mandatory reviews by top health officials and checks for 235 disfavoured terms have left hundreds of vetted grant applications in administrative limbo.

top30.title: I study World Cup penalty shoot-outs: they say a lot about the psychology of performance under pressure
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02043-0Football's most nail-biting feature can reveal ways for everyone to manage stressful situations better.

top30.title: Europe’s record heatwave: does the continent have a new climate?
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 26 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02046-xNature asks researchers whether scorching summers are the new norm for London, Paris and Berlin.

top30.title: Daily briefing: Humans and great apes giggle in the same rhythms
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 25 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02060-zThe findings hint that vocal motor control might have evolved from a shared ancestor. Plus, sea turtles’ navigation skills are only so-so and what’s next for the International Space Station.

top30.title: Oo oo, ha ha: why humans and great apes giggle alike when tickled
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 25 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01994-8The rhythmic patterns of laughter found in apes and humans reveal that complex primate vocal control might have started evolving 15 million years ago.

top30.title: US funding uncertainties threaten to sink key global oceanography projects
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 25 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02028-zThe United States has led the world in observing the oceans — but cuts and threats of cuts have researchers worried it is no longer a reliable partner.

top30.title: China’s LineShine just topped the global supercomputer ranking: what you need to know
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 25 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02047-wThe supercomputer has an innovative chip design that allows it to carry out more than two quintillion calculations per second.

top30.title: Can you terraform Mars? Try Nature’s game
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 25 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01978-8Once the realm of science fiction, the idea of making Mars habitable is gaining traction. Nature consulted with key scientists to develop this experimental adventure.

top30.title: How long-term dietary cholesterol can slow down its own clearance by liver cells
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 25 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01899-6Cholesterol carried by low-density lipoprotein (LDL) drives heart disease and is cleared by liver cells expressing the receptor LDLR. A cell-signalling mechanism has been discovered through which high cholesterol promotes the enzyme-mediated degradation of LDLR. Blocking this enzyme restores LDLR levels in liver cells, suggesting a new strategy for treating high cholesterol.

top30.title: Electric fields probe the symmetry of the ‘heavy hydrogen’ nucleus
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 25 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02036-zThe response of the nucleus of a deuterium atom to electric fields shows no evidence of asymmetry, which is consistent with conventional theories of particle physics.

top30.title: ‘Edited’ human embryos reveal secrets of our development — and fuel ethical debate
top30.abstract: Nature, Published online: 25 June 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-02027-0Ethical discussions are urgently needed as genome-editing science advances, some researchers say.
